section.title: Consecration of the Priests
section.sectionRationale: Having described the priestly garments, God now gives the ritual for actually ordaining Aaron and his sons: washing, clothing, anointing, and a sequence of sacrifices that consecrate them and the altar itself, ending with God's own stated purpose for the whole tabernacle, that He may dwell among His people.
section.thesis: God prescribes a seven day ordination ritual of washing, clothing, blood, and sacrifice to consecrate Aaron and his sons as priests, and declares that through the tabernacle and its daily offerings He will dwell among the Israelites and be their God.
section.frameNote: Four movements: washing and clothing Aaron and his sons; the sin offering, burnt offering, and ordination ram with blood applied to ear, thumb, and toe; the seven days of atonement for the altar and the priests; and the daily offerings, ending in God's declared purpose to dwell among His people.

media.alt: Priests being washed at the tent entrance
media.caption: Aaron and his sons are washed and clothed before the Tent of Meeting.

verses.text: “Now this is what you are to do to consecrate Aaron and his sons to serve Me as priests: Take a young bull and two rams without blemish,
along with unleavened bread, unleavened cakes mixed with oil, and unleavened wafers anointed with oil. Make them out of fine wheat flour,
put them in a basket, and present them in the basket, along with the bull and the two rams.
Then present Aaron and his sons at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting and wash them with water.
Take the garments and clothe Aaron with the tunic, the robe of the ephod, the ephod itself, and the breastplate. Fasten the ephod on him with its woven waistband.
Put the turban on his head and attach the holy diadem to the turban.
Then take the anointing oil and anoint him by pouring it on his head.
Present his sons as well and clothe them with tunics.
Wrap the sashes around Aaron and his sons and tie headbands on them. The priesthood shall be theirs by a permanent statute. In this way you are to ordain Aaron and his sons.

units.label: The bull, and the two rams
units.note: blood on ear, thumb, and toe

verses.text: You are to present the bull at the front of the Tent of Meeting, and Aaron and his sons are to lay their hands on its head.
And you shall slaughter the bull before the LORD at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
Take some of the blood of the bull and put it on the horns of the altar with your finger; then pour out the rest of the blood at the base of the altar.
Take all the fat that covers the entrails and the lobe of the liver, and both kidneys with the fat on them, and burn them on the altar.
But burn the flesh of the bull and its hide and dung outside the camp; it is a sin offering.
Take one of the rams, and Aaron and his sons shall lay their hands on its head.
You are to slaughter the ram, take its blood, and sprinkle it on all sides of the altar.
Cut the ram into pieces, wash the entrails and legs, and place them with its head and other pieces.
Then burn the entire ram on the altar; it is a burnt offering to the LORD, a pleasing aroma, an offering made by fire to the LORD.
Take the second ram, and Aaron and his sons are to lay their hands on its head.

Slaughter the ram, take some of its blood, and put it on the right earlobes of Aaron and his sons, on the thumbs of their right hands, and on the big toes of their right feet. Sprinkle the remaining blood on all sides of the altar.

And take some of the blood on the altar and some of the anointing oil and sprinkle it on Aaron and his garments, as well as on his sons and their garments. Then he and his garments will be {{consecrated|consecrated}}, as well as his sons and their garments.

units.label: The ordination meal
units.note: eaten by Aaron and his sons alone

verses.text: Take the fat from the ram, the fat tail, the fat covering the entrails, the lobe of the liver, both kidneys with the fat on them, and the right thigh, since this is a ram for ordination,
along with one loaf of bread, one cake of bread made with oil, and one wafer from the basket of unleavened bread that is before the LORD.
Put all these in the hands of Aaron and his sons and wave them before the LORD as a wave offering.
Then take them from their hands and burn them on the altar atop the burnt offering as a pleasing aroma before the LORD; it is an offering made by fire to the LORD.
Take the breast of the ram of Aaron's ordination and wave it before the LORD as a wave offering, and it will be your portion.
Consecrate for Aaron and his sons the breast of the wave offering that is waved and the thigh of the heave offering that is lifted up from the ram of ordination.
This will belong to Aaron and his sons as a regular portion from the Israelites, for it is the heave offering the Israelites will make to the LORD from their peace offerings.
The holy garments that belong to Aaron will belong to his sons after him, so they can be anointed and ordained in them.
The son who succeeds him as priest and enters the Tent of Meeting to minister in the Holy Place must wear them for seven days.
You are to take the ram of ordination and boil its flesh in a holy place.
At the entrance to the Tent of Meeting, Aaron and his sons are to eat the meat of the ram and the bread that is in the basket.
They must eat those things by which {{atonement|atonement}} was made for their ordination and consecration. But no outsider may eat them, because these things are sacred.
And if any of the meat of ordination or any bread is left until the morning, you are to burn up the remainder. It must not be eaten, because it is sacred.

units.label: Seven days, and the daily offering
units.note: so that I might dwell among them

media.alt: A tent glowing with light at its entrance
media.caption: God declares He will dwell among the Israelites and be their God.

verses.text: This is what you are to do for Aaron and his sons based on all that I have commanded you, taking seven days to ordain them.
Sacrifice a bull as a sin offering each day for atonement. Purify the altar by making atonement for it, and anoint it to consecrate it.
For seven days you shall make atonement for the altar and consecrate it. Then the altar will become most holy; whatever touches the altar will be holy.
This is what you are to offer regularly on the altar, each day: two lambs that are a year old.
Offer one lamb in the morning and the other at twilight.
With the first lamb offer a tenth of an ephah of fine flour, mixed with a quarter hin of oil from pressed olives, and a drink offering of a quarter hin of wine.
And offer the second lamb at twilight with the same grain offering and drink offering as in the morning, as a pleasing aroma, an offering made by fire to the LORD.
For the generations to come, this burnt offering shall be made regularly at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting before the LORD, where I will meet you to speak with you.
I will also meet with the Israelites there, and that place will be consecrated by My glory.
So I will consecrate the Tent of Meeting and the altar, and I will consecrate Aaron and his sons to serve Me as priests.
Then I will {{dwell|dwell}} among the Israelites and be their God.
And they will know that I am the LORD their God, who brought them out of the land of Egypt so that I might dwell among them. I am the LORD their God.”

consecrated.gloss: To consecrate, set apart as holy. The verb underlies the whole ordination ritual: Aaron, his sons, their garments, and even the altar itself are made qadash, set apart exclusively for God's service, no longer available for ordinary use.

atonement.gloss: To atone, to cover or make amends. Used repeatedly of blood applied to the altar and of the ordination sacrifices, this verb describes the removing of sin's barrier so that sinful priests and a sinful people could be received before a holy God.

dwell.gloss: To dwell, settle, or tabernacle. The same root behind mishkan, tabernacle. God's stated purpose for all this ordination and sacrifice is not distant ritual but that He may shakan, actually dwell, among His redeemed people as their God.

basic.context: God gives the exact ritual for ordaining Aaron and his sons as priests: washing, clothing, anointing, and a sequence of sacrifices, including blood applied to their ear, thumb, and toe, that consecrate them and even the altar itself. The chapter ends with God's own explanation of why: so that He may dwell among Israel and be their God.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a literal, historical ordination ritual for Israel's first priests, not a repeatable pattern for Christian ministry today. Its significance lies in what it reveals about atonement, consecration, and God's stated goal throughout, dwelling among His people, which the rest of Scripture develops and ultimately fulfills in Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: The whole ritual is built around qadash, to consecrate or set apart, and kaphar, to atone or cover sin, since sinful priests could not simply begin serving a holy God without both cleansing and covering. The final verses reveal the purpose behind it all: God will shakan, dwell, among His people, the same word behind mishkan, tabernacle.
basic.typology: Blood applied to ear, thumb, and toe consecrates the priest's whole self, hearing, doing, and walking, to God, anticipating the whole-life consecration believers now offer through Christ. The seven days of atonement for the altar point to the completeness and finality later found in Christ's single, sufficient sacrifice.
basic.christ: Where Aaron needed repeated washing, clothing, and blood applied to be made fit to serve, Jesus is the sinless High Priest who needed no such purification for Himself, Hebrews 7:26-27, and who consecrates believers by His own single sacrifice, Hebrews 10:10, 14.
basic.god: God's holiness demands an elaborate, costly process before sinful men can serve Him, yet the entire purpose of that process, repeated three times in the closing verses, is God's own desire to dwell among His people. Holiness and nearness are not in tension for God; His holiness is precisely what makes safe nearness possible.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 7:26-27 contrasts Christ's sinless high priesthood with priests who first had to offer for their own sins. Hebrews 10:10-14 says believers are sanctified through Christ's one offering. 1 Peter 2:5 calls believers a holy priesthood offering spiritual sacrifices.
basic.oneStory: God ordains Aaron's priesthood through blood, washing, and repeated atonement so that He might dwell among a redeemed but sinful people. That whole costly arrangement points forward to Christ, the perfect High Priest whose one sacrifice consecrates His people fully, so that God now dwells in them by His Spirit.

context.literary: This chapter moves from the initial washing and clothing of Aaron and his sons, through the layered sacrifices of the bull and two rams, to the ordination meal, and closes with a seven day pattern of atonement for the altar and God's own explanation of the whole tabernacle project's purpose.
context.historical: This ordination ritual would later be enacted in Leviticus 8, when Moses carries out exactly what is commanded here, establishing the pattern for how every subsequent high priest in Israel's history would be consecrated to office.
context.canonical: The application of blood to ear, thumb, and toe, and the entire logic of consecration through blood and atonement, becomes foundational vocabulary the New Testament draws on to describe Christ's priesthood and the sanctification of believers.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish the actual ritual by which Aaron's priesthood began, showing that access to serve a holy God requires atonement and consecration, and to declare plainly God's ultimate purpose in the tabernacle: to dwell among His redeemed people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage prescribes the specific ordination ritual for Israel's old covenant priesthood; it is not a pattern Christians reenact for ordaining ministers today. Its abiding truth is that sinful people require atonement to serve a holy God, now accomplished once for all in Christ.

debates.question: What is the significance of applying blood to the ear, thumb, and toe rather than simply sprinkling it more generally?

views.label: Symbolic consecration of the whole person
views.case: Many interpreters see the ear, hand, and foot as representing the whole range of a priest's active life, hearing God's word, doing His work, and walking in His ways, all consecrated together by this single act.

views.label: Caution against over-specific allegorizing
views.case: Others note Scripture does not explicitly explain this detail and caution against assigning too precise a meaning to each body part individually, preferring to see the act more simply as a vivid, whole-body application of the ordination blood.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm the same substantial point: the priest's entire person and conduct, not merely his office, was being set apart for God's service.

study.originalLanguages: The verbs qadash, to consecrate, and kaphar, to atone, run through the whole chapter, since Aaron and his sons could not simply assume priestly office; they had to be set apart and their sin covered first. The passage's true climax comes in the repeated use of shakan, to dwell, in the final verses, revealing that all this ritual exists so God can tabernacle among His redeemed people.

typology.type: Blood applied to the priest's ear, thumb, and toe
typology.fulfillment: The whole-life consecration of believers to God through Christ, offering their whole selves as living sacrifices, Romans 12:1.

typology.type: The daily offerings, morning and evening, before the LORD continually
typology.fulfillment: Christ's single, once for all sacrifice, which accomplishes fully what the repeated daily offerings never could, Hebrews 10:1-14.

study.christ: Hebrews contrasts Aaron's need to offer sacrifices first for his own sins and then for the people's with Jesus, who has no such need, since He offered Himself once for all, Hebrews 7:27. Where Aaron and every priest after him required repeated washing, clothing, and blood, Christ's single sacrifice permanently consecrates all who trust Him, Hebrews 10:14.
study.god: God requires an elaborate, costly process of washing, clothing, and blood before sinful men can serve Him as priests, revealing how seriously He regards His own holiness. Yet the chapter's climax is God's own repeated declaration of His purpose: to dwell among His people and be their God, showing that His holiness serves His desire for nearness, not the reverse.

crossReferences.note: Contrasts Aaron's repeated need for sacrifice with Christ's sinless, once for all high priesthood.
Believers are sanctified through the single offering of Christ's body.
Believers are built into a spiritual house, a holy priesthood offering spiritual sacrifices through Jesus Christ.

study.oneStory: Aaron's priesthood begins only through washing, clothing, and repeated blood atonement, all so that God might dwell among His redeemed but sinful people. Jesus, the perfect and final High Priest, accomplishes by His one sacrifice what all this ritual could only foreshadow, so that God now dwells permanently among His people by His Spirit.

quiz.q: What was done to Aaron and his sons before they were clothed with the priestly garments?

options.text: They were washed with water at the entrance to the Tent of Meeting.
options.note: Verse 4 describes this washing as the first step of the ordination.

options.text: They fasted for seven days beforehand.
options.note: The chapter describes washing as the preparatory step, not a preceding fast.

options.text: They were anointed before being washed.
options.note: The order given is washing first, then clothing, then anointing, not anointing before washing.

options.text: No preparatory act is described.
options.note: Verse 4 explicitly describes washing them with water first.

quiz.q: Where was the blood of the second ram applied to Aaron and his sons?

options.text: On their right earlobes, the thumbs of their right hands, and the big toes of their right feet.
options.note: Verse 20 specifies this exact application of blood.

options.text: Only on their foreheads.
options.note: The forehead held the gold plate reading Holy to the LORD, chapter 28; the ordination blood went on ear, thumb, and toe.

options.text: On their hands only.
options.note: The blood was applied to ear, thumb, and toe together, not the hands alone.

options.text: No blood was applied to their bodies.
options.note: Verse 20 explicitly describes blood applied to ear, thumb, and toe.

quiz.q: Who was permitted to eat the ordination ram and the bread from the basket?

options.text: Aaron and his sons alone, since these things were sacred.
options.note: Verses 32 to 33 restrict this meal to Aaron and his sons, forbidding any outsider.

options.text: Any Israelite who wished to participate.
options.note: Verse 33 explicitly states no outsider may eat these sacred things.

options.text: Only the seventy elders of Israel.
options.note: The meal was restricted specifically to Aaron and his sons, not the broader group of elders.

options.text: The meat and bread were burned entirely, with no one eating.
options.note: Verse 32 explicitly commands Aaron and his sons to eat the meat and bread.

quiz.q: How many days were required to complete the consecration of the altar?

options.text: Seven days, with a bull offered as a sin offering each day.
options.note: Verses 36 to 37 specify this seven day process for consecrating the altar.

options.text: One day only.
options.note: The text specifically requires seven days of ongoing atonement, not a single day.

options.text: Forty days, matching Moses' time on the mountain.
options.note: Forty days describes Moses' time on Sinai in chapter 24, not the altar's consecration period here.

options.text: No specific number of days is given.
options.note: Verse 37 explicitly specifies seven days.

quiz.q: What did God declare as His ultimate purpose for the tabernacle and its ordained priests, according to verses 45 to 46?

options.text: That He would dwell among the Israelites and be their God.
options.note: Verses 45 to 46 state this explicitly as God's stated purpose.

options.text: That Israel would become a wealthy trading nation.
options.note: No such economic purpose is given; the stated purpose is God's dwelling presence among His people.

options.text: That the priesthood would rule Israel as kings.
options.note: The priesthood served a ministering role before God, not a governing, kingly role over the nation.

options.text: No purpose is stated for the tabernacle.
options.note: Verses 45 to 46 explicitly state God's purpose to dwell among His people.

questions: Aaron could not simply step into priestly service; he had to be washed, clothed, and covered with blood first. Where in your own life have you tried to serve God without first receiving the cleansing and covering He provides in Christ?

Blood was applied to Aaron's ear, thumb, and toe, consecrating his whole self, what he heard, did, and where he walked. Which of these three areas of your life most needs to be brought under God's ownership this week?

The ordination meal was restricted to Aaron and his sons alone, a sacred and exclusive privilege. How do you currently treat the privilege of approaching God through Christ, casually or with the weight it deserves?

The chapter's real climax is God's declared purpose: 'that I may dwell among them.' How much of your view of your faith centers on rules and rituals versus centering on God's actual presence with you?

The altar itself needed seven days of atonement before it was fit for ongoing use. Where might you need patience with your own slow progress in holiness, trusting that God is still at work consecrating you over time?

translationNotes.note: In this way you are to ordain Aaron and his sons translates a Hebrew phrase literally meaning to fill the hand, an idiom for installing someone in office, likely originating from placing the priestly portion of the sacrifice directly into the ordinand's hands, as seen in verse 24.

Atonement was made for their ordination and consecration shows that even Aaron, the one who would later make atonement for others, first needed atonement made for himself, underscoring that no human priest, however honored, was without need of covering for sin.

I will dwell among the Israelites uses the same Hebrew root, shakan, that gives the tabernacle its name, mishkan, tying the entire chapter's ritual detail back to God's simple, central purpose of nearness to His people.
